title: London Diplomatic List
description: Foreign embassies, high commissions, and diplomatic missions in London
source_name: UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office — London Diplomatic List

facts: In 1967 Enver Hoxha declared Albania the world's first atheist state, banning religious practice until the 1990s.
The Albanian language is its own branch of Indo-European, not closely related to its neighbours' languages.
Britain sheltered Albania's exiled King Zog in the 1930s; London was a hub for Albanian royalist diplomacy in exile.
Algeria is Africa's largest country by land area and was French Algeria until independence after a long war in 1962.
The ancient Numidian and Roman city of Timgad in Algeria is a remarkably preserved Roman grid town.
During WWII the Allies landed in Algeria (Operation Torch); Algiers briefly hosted Free French political activity.
Angola was a Portuguese colony for centuries and gained independence in 1975 after a long liberation struggle.
The country has vast offshore oil reserves in the South Atlantic that dominate its economy.
Cuban troops fought alongside Angolan forces during the Cold War–era civil conflict.
The nation is a Commonwealth realm with the British monarch as head of state and English common-law roots.
Nelson's Dockyard in Antigua is a UNESCO site named for Horatio Nelson, who was stationed there.
Barbuda was devastated by Hurricane Irma in 2017, briefly emptying the island of residents.
Argentina and the UK fought the 1982 Falklands War over the South Atlantic islands Argentina calls Malvinas.
Buenos Aires grew rich in the late 19th century on beef and grain exports—the 'Paris of South America' era.
Tango, born in Buenos Aires port districts, blends European immigrant and Afro-Argentine influences.
Armenia was the first state to adopt Christianity as its religion, traditionally dated to AD 301.
Mount Ararat, symbol of Armenia, lies just across the border in Turkey but dominates Armenian iconography.
The Armenian diaspora is large worldwide; London hosts a long-established Armenian community and church life.
Australia began as British penal colonies; the First Fleet arrived at Sydney Cove in 1788.
Canberra was chosen as the capital to settle rivalry between Sydney and Melbourne.
ANZAC ties with Britain were forged in WWI at Gallipoli and remain central to Australian national memory.
The Habsburg monarchy ruled much of Central Europe from Vienna until the empire collapsed in 1918.
Austria declared permanent neutrality in 1955 as a condition of Allied occupation ending.
The Congress of Vienna (1814–15) redrew Europe's map after Napoleon, with Britain as a key negotiator.
Baku sits on the Caspian Sea and has been an oil centre since the 19th-century boom.
The disputed Nagorno-Karabakh region drove wars with Armenia; a 2020 conflict shifted control sharply.
Azerbaijan blends Turkic language, Shi'a-majority Islam, and Soviet-era secular habits.
The Bahamas became independent from the UK in 1973 but remains a Commonwealth realm.
Christopher Columbus made landfall in the Bahamian archipelago in 1492.
The islands' shallow banks and reefs made them a haven for pirates in the early 1700s.
Bahrain is a small island monarchy in the Gulf, linked to Saudi Arabia by the King Fahd Causeway.
Pearling once made Bahrain rich before oil; UNESCO lists historic pearling sites.
Britain signed treaties with Gulf rulers in the 19th century; Bahrain was a British protectorate until 1971.
Bangladesh emerged from the 1971 Liberation War when East Pakistan broke from West Pakistan.
The Ganges–Brahmaputra delta is among the world's largest and most densely populated river systems.
The British East India Company's Bengal Presidency was a core of colonial trade and administration.
Barbados became a republic in 2021, replacing the British monarch with a Barbadian president as head of state.
Sugar plantations worked by enslaved Africans shaped the island's economy and society for centuries.
The island is the easternmost of the Caribbean Lesser Antilles, exposed to Atlantic swells.
Minsk was heavily rebuilt in Stalinist style after WWII destruction; wide avenues define the capital.
Belarus borders three EU states and Russia, placing it at the centre of post-Soviet geopolitics.
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania once included much of today's Belarus, with Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth ties.
Belgium was created in 1830 as an independent buffer state, with British diplomacy supporting neutrality early on.
Brussels hosts the EU and NATO headquarters, making Belgium a hub of European governance.
Flemish (Dutch) and French divide the country linguistically, with a small German-speaking community.
Belize is the only Central American country where English is the official language—a British colonial legacy.
The Belize Barrier Reef is the hemisphere's longest after Australia's and is UNESCO-listed.
Guatemala long claimed Belizean territory; the dispute was settled by ICJ referral in recent years.
Bolivia is landlocked today after losing coastal territory to Chile in the War of the Pacific (1879–84).
La Paz sits in a high Andean basin; the administrative capital Sucre lies at lower elevation.
Silver from Potosí funded the Spanish Empire and reshaped global trade in the colonial era.
The 1914 assassination in Sarajevo of Archduke Franz Ferdinand helped trigger World War I.
The Dayton Agreement (1995) ended the Bosnian War and created a complex two-entity federation.
Medieval Bosnia saw the Bosnian Church and later Ottoman rule, leaving diverse religious heritage.
Botswana has been one of Africa's most stable democracies since independence from Britain in 1966.
The Okavango Delta inland floodplain is one of the planet's great wildlife wetlands.
De Beers built its diamond empire partly on Botswanan mines, with revenue shared via state partnership.
Brazil is the only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas, a legacy of the Portuguese Empire.
The Amazon rainforest covers roughly half of Brazil and is critical to global climate and biodiversity.
The UK and Brazil traded heavily in the 19th century—Brazilian coffee and British manufactures crossed the Atlantic.
Brunei is a tiny, oil-rich sultanate on Borneo ruled by a long-standing monarchy under Islamic law.
The 19th-century 'White Rajahs' of neighbouring Sarawak bordered Brunei's shrinking realm.
Bandar Seri Begawan is known for golden-domed mosques and water villages (kampong ayer).
The Cyrillic alphabet spread partly through medieval Bulgaria—Cyrillic is a UNESCO intangible heritage item.
Bulgaria joined the EU in 2007 after post-communist reforms.
The Rose Valley produces much of the world's rose oil used in perfumes.
Burundi and Rwanda share Hutu and Tutsi communities and a painful history of ethnic violence.
Bujumbura on Lake Tanganyika was the colonial capital; political capital moved inland to Gitega.
German then Belgian rule under a League of Nations mandate shaped colonial borders and administration.
Angkor Wat, built as a Hindu temple, is the world's largest religious monument by land area.
The Khmer Rouge regime (1975–79) devastated Cambodia; the country still bears deep scars.
France ruled Cambodia as part of French Indochina until independence in 1953.
Cameroon blends Anglophone and Francophone regions after German, British, and French colonial phases.
Mount Cameroon is one of Africa's largest active volcanoes, rising straight from the Gulf of Guinea.
Wildlife ranges from rainforest gorillas to savanna elephants along a strong north–south ecological gradient.
Canada is a constitutional monarchy with the British monarch as head of state and Westminster-style parliament.
The world's longest undefended border runs between Canada and the United States.
Newfoundland joined Canada in 1949; Quebec's French-speaking identity reflects French colonial roots.
Chile is thousands of kilometres long but narrow—Atacama Desert in the north, Patagonian fjords in the south.
Salvador Allende's 1973 overthrow led to Pinochet's long military rule with lasting political effects.
Nitrate from northern Chile was strategic for explosives and fertilizers before synthetic substitutes.
The Great Wall was built over centuries to defend northern frontiers; surviving Ming sections are iconic.
Britain fought the Opium Wars in the 19th century, forcing treaty ports and ceding Hong Kong until 1997.
China's high-speed rail network is the world's largest by mileage, knitting megacities together.
Bogotá sits high on a plateau in the Andes, giving it mild weather despite its tropical latitude.
Gabriel García Márquez's magical realism drew deeply on Caribbean Colombian culture.
The 2016 peace accord with the FARC aimed to end decades of internal armed conflict.
Costa Rica abolished its army in 1949, investing instead in education and health.
Cloud forests like Monteverde host astonishing biodiversity on misty mountain ridges.
British influence included banana company enclaves and Caribbean coast Afro-Costa Rican communities.
Dubrovnik was the independent merchant republic of Ragusa, a major Adriatic trading power.
Croatia fought a war of independence in the 1990s as Yugoslavia dissolved.
The Dalmatian coast's islands and Roman ruins draw millions of visitors each summer.
The 1959 revolution brought Fidel Castro to power and reshaped Cuba's relationship with the United States.
Old Havana's Spanish colonial core is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
The Spanish-American War ended Spanish rule; the US held Guantánamo Bay under a long-term lease.
Cyprus gained independence from Britain in 1960 after colonial rule since 1878 (with interruptions).
The island is divided between the Republic of Cyprus and the Turkish-backed north since 1974.
Aphrodite's mythical birthplace is marked at Petra tou Romiou on the south coast.
Prague's Charles University, founded 1348, is one of Europe's oldest universities.
The Velvet Revolution of 1989 ended communist rule peacefully in what was then Czechoslovakia.
Czechoslovakia was carved out of the Austro-Hungarian Empire after World War I, with strong British wartime ties to exiles.
Abidjan is West Africa's economic hub though the political capital is Yamoussoukro.
The country became independent from France in 1960 under Félix Houphouët-Boigny's long leadership.
Ivory Coast (French name) reflects historic elephant ivory trade along forest routes.
The Congo River is among the world's largest by discharge and drains a vast central African basin.
The Belgian Congo under Leopold II became infamous for rubber extraction and brutal exploitation.
UN peacekeeping, including UK contributions, has operated amid recurring eastern instability.
Greenland and the Faroe Islands remain autonomous territories within the Danish Realm.
The Viking Age linked Danish seafarers to Britain—Danelaw settlements shaped English place names.
Copenhagen's cycling infrastructure and design culture are global references for livable cities.
Dominica is called the 'Nature Island' for rainforests, hot springs, and the boiling Lake hike.
It is not the Dominican Republic—Dominica is smaller, English-speaking, and between Guadeloupe and Martinique.
Kalinago (Carib) communities remain more prominent here than on many Caribbean islands.
The DR shares Hispaniola with Haiti; colonial Santo Domingo claims the Americas' oldest European city layout.
Merengue and bachata music genres are central to Dominican cultural identity.
Trujillo's dictatorship (1930–61) was one of the Caribbean's harshest autocracies.
The equator runs through Ecuador—monuments near Quito mark the line (with slight survey quirks).
The Galápagos Islands, part of Ecuador, inspired Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection.
Quito is one of the world's highest capitals, tucked in an Andean valley beneath volcanic peaks.
The Suez Canal, opened in 1869, links Europe and Asia; the 1956 crisis drew in Britain, France, and Israel.
Pharaonic monuments from Giza to Luxor anchor one of history's longest continuous civilizations.
British occupation ran from 1882 until gradual independence; the 1922 kingdom kept British influence for years.
El Salvador is Central America's smallest country but among its most densely populated.
The 1980s civil war killed tens of thousands; a 1992 peace accord ended the fighting.
Bitcoin was adopted as legal tender in 2021—a controversial monetary experiment.
Malabo, the capital, sits on Bioko island off the mainland, a legacy of Spanish colonial geography.
Oil wealth contrasts with persistent inequality and limited political pluralism.
Spanish is the official language—rare in sub-Saharan Africa outside former Spanish territories.
Eritrea fought a 30-year war to secede from Ethiopia, gaining independence in 1993 after a federation failed.
Massawa and Asmara reflect Italian colonial architecture—Asmara is a UNESCO modernist site.
The Red Sea coast made Eritrea strategically important to empires and Cold War rivals.
Estonia restored independence in 1991 as the USSR dissolved; the Singing Revolution used mass song festivals.
Tallinn's medieval Old Town is among Europe's best-preserved Hanseatic cores.
The country is a digital-government pioneer—e-residency and online voting are signature policies.
Eswatini (formerly Swaziland) is one of the world's last absolute monarchies in Africa.
The king renamed the country in 2018 to reflect the Swazi nation.
British protectorate status ended at independence in 1968; English remains an official language.
Ethiopia was never colonised in the Scramble for Africa—Italy's occupation (1936–41) was reversed.
Lucy, the famous Australopithecus fossil, was found in Ethiopia's Afar region.
The Ethiopian Orthodox Church claims ancient roots linking to early Christianity in the Horn.
Fiji's iTaukei (indigenous) culture and large Indian-Fijian community reflect colonial labour migration.
Britain ceded Fiji in 1970; the Great Council of Chiefs once played a formal role under the constitution.
Some 300 islands span Melanesia and Polynesia culturally, with rugby as a national passion.
Finland fought the Winter War (1939–40) against Soviet invasion, becoming a symbol of stubborn resistance.
Sauna culture is UNESCO-listed; lakes and forests define the national landscape.
The UK fought alongside Finland's neighbour Sweden in the Napoleonic era, while Finland was later part of Russia until 1917.
The Channel Tunnel (1994) physically links France and the UK beneath the English Channel.
The French Revolution reshaped global politics; its ideals still echo in rights-based discourse.
Louisiana Purchase territory once belonged to France, shaping North America's map before U.S. expansion.
Gabon is heavily forested; national parks protect lowland gorillas and forest elephants.
It gained independence from France in 1960; Omar Bongo ruled for over four decades.
Libreville was founded by freed people—its name means 'free town' in French.
The Gambia is a thin enclave along the Gambia River, surrounded by Senegal except at the coast.
James Island (Kunta Kinteh Island) was a key Atlantic slave-trade site, now a memorial UNESCO site.
British colonial rule ended at independence in 1965; the country briefly left the Commonwealth in 2013 before rejoining.
Georgia claims to be the birthplace of wine—archaeological evidence of early viniculture is famous.
The Caucasus Mountains divide Europe and Asia; Georgia sits on ancient Silk Road routes.
The UK supports Georgia's Euro-Atlantic aspirations and territorial integrity after the 2008 Russia conflict.
German unification in 1871 created a new great power in Central Europe under Prussian leadership.
Berlin's Wall (1961–89) symbolised the Cold War; its fall in 1989 preceded rapid reunification.
The UK and Germany are major EU trading partners despite Brexit shifting the institutional framework.
Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African colony to gain independence, in 1957 under Kwame Nkrumah.
Medieval Ghana is unrelated geographically—modern Ghana took the name as a Pan-African symbol.
Gold Coast trade drew European forts; Cape Coast Castle held enslaved people before the Middle Passage.
Ancient Athens incubated democracy, philosophy, and drama that still shape Western thought.
Greece fought a war of independence in the 1820s; British philhellenes and loans aided the cause.
Thousands of islands dot the Aegean and Ionian seas, each with distinct local traditions.
The 1983 U.S.-led invasion followed a coup and concerned Cold War powers—including UK unease over precedents.
Grenada is the 'Isle of Spice' for nutmeg and mace production.
Hurricane Ivan in 2004 devastated nutmeg groves, reshaping the rural economy for years.
Tikal's Maya pyramids rise above the Petén rainforest—one of the classic civilization's great city-states.
A 36-year internal conflict ended in 1996; truth commissions documented mass atrocities against Indigenous Maya.
Lake Atitlán sits in a volcanic caldera ringed by Maya towns and steep peaks.
Guinea was the first French West African colony to vote for independence, under Sékou Touré in 1958.
The country holds major bauxite reserves, feeding global aluminium supply chains.
Mount Nimba, shared with Côte d'Ivoire, is a biodiversity hotspot with unique endemic species.
Guyana is South America's only English-speaking mainland country and the continent's only Commonwealth realm.
Kaieteur Falls on the Potaro River is one of the world's highest single-drop waterfalls by volume.
The 1899 Paris arbitration settled the border with Venezuela, a settlement Venezuela still disputes rhetorically.
Haiti was the first independent nation in Latin America and the Caribbean, and the world's first Black-led republic, after its 1804 revolution against France.
The Citadelle Laferrière, a mountaintop fortress built after independence, is a UNESCO World Heritage site and symbol of Haitian resistance.
Britain briefly occupied parts of Haiti during the Napoleonic Wars era; today the UK maintains diplomatic relations through its embassy in Santo Domingo covering Haiti.
The Holy See is the sovereign entity governing Vatican City and conducts diplomacy as a distinct subject of international law, not merely as a religious office.
It maintains diplomatic relations with the United Kingdom, which exchanged full ambassadorial-level ties in the 1980s after centuries of complex history.
St. Peter's Basilica sits on a site linked to early Christian Rome; the Vatican Library holds manuscripts spanning over a millennium of European and global history.
Honduras is named after the deep waters ("honduras") Spanish explorers encountered off its Caribbean coast.
The ancient Maya city of Copán, in western Honduras, is renowned for its sculpted stelae and hieroglyphic stairway.
The UK is a trading partner and supports development and governance projects through its embassy in Guatemala City, which covers Honduras.
Hungary's Parliament building on the Danube in Budapest is one of Europe's largest legislative structures and a neo-Gothic landmark.
The 1956 Hungarian Uprising against Soviet rule became a defining Cold War moment; refugees were welcomed in several Western countries, including the UK.
Hungarian is a Finno-Ugric language unrelated to its Slavic, Germanic, or Romance neighbors, reflecting distinct migration history.
Iceland's Althing, founded in 930 CE, is often cited as one of the world's oldest surviving parliaments.
The island sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, making it a hotspot for volcanoes, geysers, and geothermal power.
During the Second World War British forces occupied Iceland to deny it to Axis powers, shaping Iceland's path toward independence from Denmark.
The British East India Company and later the Crown ruled much of the subcontinent until independence and partition in 1947.
India is the world's most populous country and has dozens of officially recognized languages, with Hindi and English prominent at the national level.
The Himalayas form India's northern frontier and include Kanchenjunga, among the world's highest peaks, on the border with Nepal.
Indonesia is the world's largest archipelagic state, straddling the equator across more than 17,000 islands.
It was a Dutch colony until 1945; the UK briefly administered parts of Java and Sumatra after Japanese occupation in the post-war transition.
Borneo, Java, Sulawesi, and Sumatra anchor enormous biodiversity, including orangutans and the Wallace Line biogeographic divide.
Persia was a major empire for millennia; Reza Shah and later his son reshaped the modern state, renamed Iran in 1935.
The 1979 revolution replaced the monarchy with an Islamic republic, transforming Iran's domestic and foreign policy.
Ancient Persepolis near Shiraz preserves monumental Achaemenid ruins from the era of Cyrus and Darius.
Mesopotamia—"land between the rivers"—saw the rise of Sumer, Babylon, and Assyria, often called the cradle of writing and cities.
Britain held a League of Nations mandate over Iraq after World War I, shaping borders and institutions before independence in 1932.
The Tigris and Euphrates define Iraq's geography from Kurdish highlands in the north to marshlands and deserts in the south.
Ireland gained independence from the UK in stages; Northern Ireland remained in the United Kingdom, defining the island's modern partition.
The Good Friday Agreement (1998) helped end much of the Troubles and established complex North–South and British–Irish institutions.
Ireland is the only EU member with a land border with the UK, a focus of Brexit negotiations.
Israel was established in 1948 following a UN partition plan and war, with borders and status contested regionally ever since.
Jerusalem hosts sites sacred to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, making the city's governance a focal point of diplomacy.
The UK held the Palestine Mandate after World War I, and British policy in the mandate period still shapes historical narratives today.
Rome was the heart of an empire that left law, roads, and language across Europe; Italy unified as a nation-state only in the 19th century.
Venice and Genoa were maritime republics whose trade networks linked the Mediterranean to Northern Europe for centuries.
The UK and Italy were allies in both world wars—on opposite sides in the first and the same side in the second after Italy's 1943 armistice and split.
Jamaica became independent from the United Kingdom in 1962 but remains a Commonwealth realm with the British monarch as head of state.
The island's Blue Mountains produce renowned coffee, while its music—notably reggae—became globally influential.
Spanish Town and Port Royal reflect layers of colonial history before British rule solidified Jamaica as a major sugar colony.
Japan remained largely closed to foreigners during the Tokugawa shogunate until U.S. Commodore Perry's 1853 expedition helped end isolation.
The UK was Japan's ally from 1902 to 1923 under the Anglo-Japanese Alliance, a landmark Asian–European pact.
Japan is an archipelago on the Pacific Ring of Fire, experiencing frequent earthquakes and nurturing a rich Shinto and Buddhist cultural heritage.
The Hashemite Kingdom emerged from post-Ottoman diplomacy; Transjordan gained independence with British ties before evolving into today's Jordan.
Petra, carved into rose-red sandstone by the Nabataeans, draws visitors from around the world and is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
Jordan hosts large numbers of Palestinian and Syrian refugees, placing humanitarian and diplomatic issues at the center of state policy.
Kazakhstan is the world's largest landlocked country and spans Central Asia from the Caspian steppe toward the Altai mountains.
It inherited the Soviet space launch complex at Baikonur, still central to Russian crewed spaceflight.
Nomadic horse culture and the Silk Road history shaped Kazakh identity before Russian expansion and later Soviet rule.
Kenya's highlands and Great Rift Valley cradle early hominin fossils that rewrote understanding of human evolution.
It gained independence from Britain in 1963; English remains widely used in government and business alongside Swahili.
Maasai Mara and other reserves anchor East Africa's safari tourism and transboundary wildlife conservation.
Kosovo declared independence from Serbia in 2008; recognition remains divided among UN member states, including five EU members that do not recognize it.
Medieval Serbian Orthodox monasteries in Kosovo are UNESCO-listed, reflecting contested historical narratives.
The UK was a leading NATO participant in the 1999 Kosovo campaign and recognizes Kosovo's independence.
Kuwait's oil wealth transformed a small Gulf sheikhdom into one of the world's highest per-capita income states.
Iraq's 1990 invasion led to the Gulf War; British forces were major contributors to the US-led coalition that liberated Kuwait.
Before oil, pearling and trade across the Gulf shaped Kuwaiti society and ties to India and Britain.
Kyrgyzstan is mountainous Kyrgyz Ala-Too country, with Issyk-Kul—one of the world's largest alpine lakes—a centerpiece of its geography.
The Epic of Manas, among the longest oral epics, remains central to Kyrgyz cultural identity.
It gained independence with the Soviet collapse in 1991 and has navigated between Russian influence and broader diplomatic partnerships.
Laos is the only landlocked country in Southeast Asia; the Mekong River is its lifeline for transport and agriculture.
The Plain of Jars holds thousands of ancient stone jars whose origins are still studied by archaeologists.
During the Vietnam War era, Laos was heavily bombed in covert campaigns; unexploded ordnance clearance continues today.
Riga's art nouveau architecture is among Europe's finest, reflecting prosperity before World War I in the Baltic provinces.
Latvia was independent between the wars, annexed by the USSR in 1940, and regained independence in 1991 as the Soviet Union dissolved.
It joined NATO and the EU in 2004, anchoring its security and economy in Western institutions alongside Estonia and Lithuania.
Beirut was long called the "Paris of the Middle East" for its cosmopolitan culture before civil war and recurring crises reshaped the city.
Lebanon's confessional political system allocates offices among religious communities, a legacy of French mandate-era arrangements.
Cedars of Lebanon appear in ancient texts; today small groves survive as national symbols on the flag.
Lesotho is an enclave entirely inside South Africa, one of the world's few true enclave states.
Its mountainous terrain helped the Basuto people preserve autonomy during the Mfecane and colonial era.
It was Basutoland under British protection before independence in 1966; the UK still supports development through aid and Commonwealth ties.
Liberia was founded in the 19th century with support from the American Colonization Society as a home for free African Americans and recaptured persons.
Its capital Monrovia honors U.S. President James Monroe; English is the official language.
Firestone's rubber plantations linked Liberia to American industry for decades, shaping its economy and politics.
Ancient Tripolitania and Cyrenaica were Roman and later Byzantine provinces; Leptis Magna preserves spectacular ruins on the coast.
Italy colonized Libya until World War II; Britain administered Tripolitania and Cyrenaica in the post-war transition.
Oil transformed Libya after independence in 1951; the 2011 uprising ended decades of Qadhafi's rule.
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania once stretched from the Baltic deep into today's Belarus and Ukraine, forming a major medieval power with Poland.
Vilnius boasts a baroque old town and was a center of Jewish learning before the Holocaust erased most of that community.
Lithuania was the first Soviet republic to declare independence in 1990, helping trigger the USSR's dissolution.
Luxembourg is a founding member of what became the EU and hosts several EU institutions and agencies.
Fortress Luxembourg earned the nickname "Gibraltar of the North" before late-19th-century dismantling of fortifications.
It is a constitutional monarchy with a Grand Duke; steel and then finance built its modern prosperity.
Madagascar broke from Africa and India geologically long ago, evolving unique lemurs and baobabs found nowhere else naturally.
Malagasy culture blends Austronesian settlement from across the Indian Ocean with African influences.
France ruled Madagascar as a colony until independence in 1960; the UK trades and partners on conservation and development.
Lake Malawi—Africa's third-largest lake—defines the country's eastern border and extraordinary freshwater biodiversity.
It was Nyasaland under British colonial rule before independence in 1964 led by Hastings Banda.
The country is long and narrow, with highlands separating the lake from Mozambique and Zambia.
Malaysia combines peninsular states with Sabah and Sarawak on Borneo, separated by the South China Sea.
British Malaya and later Malaysia shaped parliamentary institutions; the UK remains an important trade and education partner.
The Strait of Malacca is one of the world's busiest shipping channels, flanking Malaysia's west coast.
The Maldives is Asia's smallest country by land area, spread across coral atolls in the Indian Ocean.
Its highest natural point is only about two meters above sea level, making sea-level rise an existential diplomatic issue.
It was a British protectorate until 1965 and is now a Muslim-majority republic heavily reliant on tourism and fishing.
Malta's strategic position in the Mediterranean led to centuries of rule by knights, France, and Britain before independence in 1964.
The George Cross was awarded collectively to Malta for bravery under siege in World War II; it appears on the national flag.
Maltese is a Semitic language written in Latin script, reflecting Arab and European layers of history.

embassies.address: Carlyle House, 235–237 Vauxhall Bridge Road, SW1V 1EJ
facts: Much of Mauritania lies in the Sahara; ancient caravan routes crossed its sands linking West Africa to the Mediterranean.
It was the last country globally to abolish slavery legally, though forced labor and descent-based servitude remain human rights concerns.
Nouakchott, the capital, grew rapidly from a village after independence from France in 1960.
Mauritius was uninhabited before European arrival; Dutch, French, and British rule shaped a diverse, multilingual society.
The dodo, extinct by the late 17th century, was endemic to Mauritius and became an icon of human-caused extinction.
The UK retains the Chagos Archipelago as the British Indian Ocean Territory—a long-running sovereignty dispute with Mauritius.
Mexico's War of Independence ended Spanish rule in 1821; the 1846–48 war with the U.S. redrew North American borders.
It is extraordinarily biodiverse, bridging temperate and tropical zones from Sonoran deserts to Chiapas rainforests.
The UK and Mexico maintain strong trade ties; London was Latin America's first bond market for independent Mexico in the 1820s.
Moldova sits between Romania and Ukraine; Romanian is the official language, reflecting shared history with Moldavia west of the Prut.
Transnistria broke away in a 1990s conflict and hosts Russian troops, freezing a "frozen conflict" on the Dniester.
Wine cellars in Mileștii Mici hold some of the world's largest underground wine collections.
Monaco is a tiny city-state on the French Riviera, ruled almost continuously by the Grimaldi family since the 13th century.
It is densely built on steep terrain and known for the Grand Prix circuit threading its streets.
The UK and Monaco cooperate on tax information exchange and financial regulation within broader European frameworks.
The Mongol Empire under Genghis Khan and his successors became the largest contiguous land empire in history.
Mongolia is landlocked between Russia and China, with vast steppes sustaining nomadic pastoral traditions.
Outer Mongolia was under Qing rule, then Soviet influence; it became a multiparty democracy in the 1990s.
Montenegro resisted full Ottoman incorporation for centuries and was recognized as independent at the Congress of Berlin in 1878.
Its Adriatic coast includes medieval Kotor, a UNESCO-listed bay ringed by mountains.
It peacefully separated from Serbia in 2006 and joined NATO in 2017, pursuing EU membership.
Morocco spans the Atlas Mountains, the Sahara, and both Atlantic and Mediterranean coastlines, near the Strait of Gibraltar.
It was a French and Spanish protectorate before independence in 1956; Western Sahara remains disputed internationally.
The UK and Morocco have centuries of diplomatic contact, including treaties on trade and piracy in earlier eras.
Mozambique's long Indian Ocean coast made it a hub in the Indian Ocean trade and later Portuguese colonial rule.
It gained independence in 1975; a long civil war ended with a 1992 peace deal brokered partly with international support.
The UK is a development partner and investor, particularly in energy and extractives.
Bagan's plain holds thousands of Buddhist temples built when Burmese kingdoms flourished along the Irrawaddy.
Britain annexed Burma in stages during the 19th century; independence came in 1948.
The 2021 military coup reversed a decade of partial opening, triggering widespread resistance and international sanctions.
The Namib Desert is among the world's oldest; the Skeleton Coast is notorious for fog and shipwrecks.
Germany colonized the area before South African rule under a League of Nations mandate; independence arrived in 1990 after a long liberation struggle.
The UK supported Namibian independence diplomatically through the UN and Commonwealth engagement.
Nepal is home to eight of the world's fourteen 8,000-meter peaks, including Everest on its border with China.
It was never colonized, though the Anglo-Nepalese War led to a treaty shaping its relations with British India.
The Kathmandu Valley's Newar architecture and Hindu-Buddhist heritage anchor its UNESCO sites.
The Dutch Republic was a 17th-century maritime and financial powerhouse whose merchants and fleets reached Asia, Africa, and the Americas.
Much of the country lies below sea level, defended by dikes, pumps, and centuries of reclaimed polders.
William of Orange became King of England, Scotland, and Ireland in 1689, tying Dutch and British crowns in the Glorious Revolution.
The Treaty of Waitangi (1840) between Māori chiefs and the British Crown remains central to New Zealand law and politics.
New Zealand was the first country to grant women the right to vote in national elections, in 1893.
Its isolation fostered unique wildlife—kiwi, kākāpō, and tuatara—before human settlement and introduced species reshaped ecosystems.
Nigeria unites hundreds of ethnic groups; Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, and Igbo are the largest, with English as the official language from British colonial rule.
It gained independence in 1960; the 1967–70 Biafran civil war killed hundreds of thousands and still shapes politics.
Nollywood is one of the world's largest film industries by output, exporting African stories globally.
Korea was divided after World War II; the DPRK was proclaimed in 1948 under Kim Il-sung with Soviet backing.
The 1950–53 Korean War ended in an armistice, not a peace treaty, leaving a heavily fortified border with South Korea.
The UK established diplomatic relations in 2000 but maintains critical human rights reporting and non-proliferation concerns.
The country renamed itself North Macedonia in 2019 to resolve a decades-long dispute with Greece over the name "Macedonia."
Ohrid on Lake Ohrid hosts Byzantine churches and is a UNESCO site shared with Albania.
Alexander the Great's ancient kingdom lies mostly in Greece; modern North Macedonia traces Slavic settlement from the medieval period.
The Viking Age saw Norse seafarers reach Iceland, Greenland, and briefly North America long before Columbus.
Norway dissolved its union with Sweden peacefully in 1905 and later resisted Nazi occupation with help from the UK government in exile.
Its fjords, oil fund sovereign wealth, and Arctic coastline make it a key NATO member with a long maritime tradition.
Oman built a maritime empire stretching to East Africa; Zanzibar was long tied to Omani rule before separation.
The UK had a close treaty relationship with the Sultanate for much of the 20th century, including military cooperation.
Muscat sits between mountains and sea; frankincense from Dhofar linked Oman to ancient trade routes.
Pakistan emerged from the 1947 partition of British India, one of the largest mass migrations in history amid horrific violence.
It controls part of Kashmir disputed with India; both countries are nuclear-armed, raising stakes for diplomacy.
The Indus Valley civilization sites of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa lie mainly in Pakistan's south.
The British Mandate for Palestine (1920–48) set the stage for competing Jewish and Arab national claims.
Palestinian aspirations are represented by the PLO and, since 2012, non-member observer state status at the UN.
Jerusalem, Gaza, and the West Bank are focal points of Israeli–Palestinian negotiations and international law debates.
The Panama Canal, opened by the U.S. in 1914, was transferred to Panama in 1999 and remains a linchpin of global shipping.
Panama broke from Colombia in 1903 with U.S. support, enabling canal treaty negotiations.
The UK uses the canal for trade and has financial and maritime links through London's role in shipping services.
PNG occupies the eastern half of New Guinea plus islands; it is among the world's most linguistically diverse countries.
It was administered by Australia under a UN trusteeship after Japanese occupation in World War II.
Highland agriculture developed taro and sweet potato cultures independently for millennia before outside contact.
Paraguay fought the devastating War of the Triple Alliance (1864–70) against Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay, losing huge swaths of population.
It is one of two landlocked countries in South America (with Bolivia) and relies on river systems for trade.
Guaraní is an official language alongside Spanish, rare in the Americas for indigenous co-official status at national level.
The Inca Empire united the Andes before Spanish conquest; Machu Picchu symbolizes its engineering and terrace agriculture.
Peru was the center of Spain's silver wealth extracted from Potosí's orbit and Cerro de Pasco mines.
British investment built key Peruvian railways in the 19th century to move guano and minerals to the coast.
The Philippines was a Spanish colony for over 300 years, then U.S. territory until independence in 1946.
It is an archipelago of over 7,000 islands on the Pacific Ring of Fire, exposed to typhoons and earthquakes.
The UK and Philippines cooperate on maritime security, trade, and development in the Indo-Pacific.
The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth was once Europe's largest state before partitions erased Poland from the map for 123 years.
Britain pledged to defend Poland in 1939, entering World War II when Germany invaded; Polish pilots fought in the Battle of Britain.
Solidarity's 1980s strikes helped undermine communist rule, contributing to the 1989 revolutions in Eastern Europe.
Portugal's 15th–16th-century navigators opened sea routes to India, Brazil, and beyond, launching the global Portuguese empire.
The Anglo-Portuguese Alliance, dating to the 14th century, is often cited as the oldest still-standing diplomatic alliance.
Lisbon's 1755 earthquake, tsunami, and fires reshaped urban planning and European thought about natural disasters.
Once a pearling economy, Qatar became the world's largest LNG exporter and hosted the first FIFA World Cup in the Arab world in 2022.
The UK and Qatar have long-standing defence and trade ties, including joint exercises and major Qatari investment in British assets.
Al Jazeera, founded in Doha in 1996, reshaped Arabic-language news and global perceptions of the Gulf.
Brazzaville faces Kinshasa across the Congo River—two national capitals closer than almost any other pair worldwide.
French is the official language, a legacy of colonial rule that still shapes education, law, and regional diplomacy.
Dense rainforests cover much of the country, hosting lowland gorillas and critical carbon stocks for the planet.
The 1878 Congress of Berlin recognised Romanian independence; Romania sided with the Allies in both world wars after shifting alliances in WWI.
Transylvania's castles and Saxon fortified churches blend Hungarian, German, and Romanian heritage across the Carpathians.
Romania is home to much of the Danube Delta, one of Europe's largest wetlands and a UNESCO biosphere reserve.
Spanning eleven time zones, Russia is by far the world's largest country by land area.
The Great Northern War and Napoleonic campaigns cemented Russia as a European great power; the UK often balanced against or allied with it in shifting coalitions.
Lake Baikal holds about one-fifth of the world's unfrozen fresh surface water and is Earth's deepest lake.
Rwanda is one of Africa's most densely populated mainland countries, with steep green hills terraced for farming.
Kinyarwanda, French, English, and Swahili reflect layered colonial and regional history in public life.
Gorilla tourism in the Virunga foothills funds conservation and has helped rebuild Rwanda's global image after the 1994 genocide.
The Federation is the Western Hemisphere's smallest sovereign state by land area and the only one named for two islands.
Sugar plantations shaped its economy for centuries; independence from the UK came in 1983 within the Commonwealth.
The narrow channel between the islands is nicknamed 'The Narrows' and was once bridged by a carrying trade in enslaved people—today it hosts a scenic ferry.
The Pitons—two volcanic spires—are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and emblem of the island.
Saint Lucia changed hands between France and Britain fourteen times before British rule stabilised; French Creole culture endures.
The island is a leading Eastern Caribbean cruise and yachting hub, with a strong banana and now tourism economy.
The nation is an archipelago of one main island and dozens of smaller Grenadines, including exclusive resort islets.
La Soufrière volcano on Saint Vincent erupted as recently as 2021, displacing thousands and coating farmland in ash.
The UK granted independence in 1979; the country remains a Commonwealth realm with the British monarch as head of state.
The kingdom contains Islam's two holiest cities, Mecca and Medina, drawing millions of pilgrims annually.
Aramco's 2019 IPO was among the largest ever, underscoring oil's centrality to state finances and global energy markets.
Vision 2030 aims to diversify beyond hydrocarbons with megaprojects like NEOM and expanded tourism visas.
Dakar hosted the final of the transatlantic slave trade era at Gorée Island, now a memorial visited by world leaders.
Senegal is often cited as one of West Africa's most stable democracies, with peaceful power transfers.
The UK has growing trade and security cooperation with Senegal, including maritime and development partnerships.
Belgrade sits where the Sava meets the Danube, a strategic crossroads contested since Roman Singidunum.
Serbia was the largest republic in Yugoslavia; the wars of the 1990s and Kosovo's 2008 declaration still shape diplomacy.
Serbian Orthodox monasteries like Studenica and Gračanica are UNESCO-listed medieval masterpieces.
This Indian Ocean archipelago has the smallest population of any African UN member state.
Aldabra, one of the world's largest raised coral atolls, hosts giant tortoises and is a UNESCO site.
The UK ceded the islands to independence in 1976; Creole, French, and English reflect layered colonial history.
Freetown was founded as a haven for liberated Africans and Black Loyalists from Nova Scotia, giving Sierra Leone a unique diaspora origin story.
Blood diamonds fuelled a brutal civil war (1991–2002); the Kimberley Process emerged partly in response.
Tacugama Chimpanzee Sanctuary near Freetown protects endangered primates in misty Western Area forests.
Expelled from Malaysia in 1965, Singapore built a global hub port and air bridge with few natural resources.
English is a lingua franca alongside Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—legacy of British rule and multicultural policy.
The UK and Singapore maintain a strong defence arrangement and deep financial and legal ties in the Commonwealth tradition.
Bratislava uniquely borders two countries—Austria and Hungary—making it a compact Central European capital.
The peaceful 1993 'Velvet Divorce' split Czechoslovakia; Slovakia joined the EU and NATO in 2004.
The High Tatras offer Slovakia's highest peaks and alpine lakes between Poland and Ukraine.
Lake Bled's island church and cliffside castle are icons of a country smaller than Wales but packed with Alps and karst.
Slovenia was the wealthiest Yugoslav republic and the first to secede in 1991 after a brief conflict.
The UK was among early recognisers of Slovenian independence and is now a close EU partner.
Ancient city-states on the Somali coast traded frankincense and myrrh with Rome, Persia, and India.
The failed 1993 UN humanitarian mission in Mogadishu became a turning point in Western military intervention doctrine.
Somalia has Africa's longest coastline on the Indian Ocean and Gulf of Aden, central to piracy debates in the 2000s.

embassies.address: South Africa House, Trafalgar Square, WC2N 5DP
facts: The Union of South Africa (1910) united British colonies and Boer republics; apartheid ended with democratic elections in 1994.
The UK is South Africa's largest European investor and a major source of tourists to Cape Town and the wine lands.
The Cape Floral Region is one of the world's biodiversity hotspots, with thousands of plant species found nowhere else.
The 1953 armistice left Korea divided; the Demilitarized Zone is both a tense border and an accidental nature reserve.
From post-war ruin, South Korea became a semiconductor, shipbuilding, and pop-culture powerhouse (the 'Korean Wave').
British forces fought under UN command in the Korean War; today the UK and ROK cooperate on trade and security in the Indo-Pacific.
South Sudan became the world's newest country in 2011 after Africa's longest civil war, yet conflict has persisted.
The Sudd is one of Earth's largest wetlands, a vast seasonal swamp on the White Nile vital for pastoralists and wildlife.
Juba lies near the equator but sits on a plateau—nights can be surprisingly cool for the tropics.
The 1492 fall of Granada ended centuries of Al-Andalus rule and launched an age of global Spanish empire.
The UK still holds Gibraltar at the strait's mouth—a sovereignty issue that has flared across EU and NATO cooperation.
Spain is the EU's second-largest country by area and receives more international tourists than almost any other state.
Ancient Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa were Buddhist civilisation centres; colonial Portuguese, Dutch, and British layers followed.
Ceylon tea became a British plantation staple; independence in 1948 retained Commonwealth ties.
Sri Lanka's biodiversity includes elephants, leopards, and Sinharaja rainforest—a UNESCO site in the island's wet zone.
Khartoum sits at the confluence of the White and Blue Niles, a historic trade and imperial choke point.
Anglo-Egyptian condominium rule (1899–1956) shaped Sudan's north–south dynamics long before South Sudan's secession.
Pyramids at Meroë rival Egypt's in number, relics of the Kingdom of Kush along the Nile.
The 17th-century Swedish Empire stretched around the Baltic until defeats to Russia reshaped the map.
Sweden has not fought a war since 1814, pursuing armed neutrality before joining NATO in 2024.
Stockholm's archipelago has tens of thousands of islands, a defining feature of Swedish summer life.
Permanent neutrality and banking secrecy made Switzerland a diplomatic venue—the League of Nations and many UN agencies sit in Geneva.
The Alps cover about three-fifths of the country, with iconic peaks like the Matterhorn straddling the Italian border.
The UK and Switzerland negotiate bespoke trade arrangements outside the EU single market.
Damascus is among the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, with layers from Aramaean to Ottoman rule.
The French Mandate drew borders that later shaped Lebanon and Syria's modern state.
Palmyra's ruins in the desert once linked Roman and Persian worlds—tragically damaged during recent war.
Over ninety percent of Tajikistan is mountainous; the Pamirs host remote communities and epic trekking routes.
It was the poorest Soviet republic; civil war after independence devastated infrastructure in the 1990s.
Persian culture persists in Tajik, unlike Turkic neighbours—Samarkand and Bukhara historically tied the region together.
Tanganyika and Zanzibar merged in 1964 to form Tanzania; Julius Nyerere championed African socialism and Swahili nation-building.
Serengeti migrations and Kilimanjaro make Tanzania a flagship safari and climbing destination.
The UK was the colonial power in Tanganyika; Dar es Salaam remains the largest Swahili-speaking city.
Siam was never colonised, navigating British and French imperial pressure through skillful diplomacy and border adjustments.
Bangkok's Grand Palace and Buddhist temples contrast with a modern manufacturing hub—the 'Detroit of Asia' for vehicles.
Southern isthmus rainforests and Andaman islands anchor tourism alongside the historic kingdom of Ayutthaya.
A 1999 independence referendum ended Indonesian rule; UN peacekeepers—including UK personnel—helped stabilise the transition.
Tetum and Portuguese are official, reflecting resistance-era identity and Portuguese colonial history.
Oil and gas in the Timor Sea fund the state but raise questions about long-term sustainability after fields peak.
Lomé became a major West African port under German, then French rule; Togo gained independence from France in 1960.
The country is narrow north–south, stretching from Atlantic beaches to savanna highlands near Burkina Faso.
The UK had little direct colonial rule here but trades with Togo through EU–ACP frameworks and Commonwealth West African links.
Tonga was never fully colonised; it became a protected state and unique Pacific kingdom with a hereditary nobility.
The 2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Ha'apai eruption was one of the largest atmospheric explosions in the satellite era.
The UK was the protecting power until 1970; Tonga remains a Commonwealth member with close ties to New Zealand and Australia.
The southernmost Caribbean nation sits near Venezuela's coast; its energy wealth funds Carnival and steelpan culture.
Indentured South Asian labour after slavery created one of the hemisphere's most diverse populations.
Independence from the UK came in 1962; the country is a republic within the Commonwealth.
Ancient Carthage rivalled Rome until the Punic Wars; Roman ruins at El Djem include a colossal amphitheatre.
The 2011 Jasmine Revolution sparked the Arab Spring, ousting a long-ruling autocrat.
The UK supported Tunisian transition with aid and trade; the Medina of Tunis is a UNESCO maze of souks and mosques.
Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia across the Bosporus, successor to Byzantine Constantinople and Ottoman grandeur.
The UK fought Turkey at Gallipoli in WWI but later partnered in NATO; EU accession talks stalled for years.
Göbekli Tepe predates Stonehenge by millennia, rewriting assumptions about the origins of monumental religion.
The Karakum Desert covers most of the country; the Darvaza gas crater has burned since a 1971 Soviet drilling mishap.
Ashgabat's white marble facades and Guinness records reflect an eccentric post-Soviet personality cult.
Ancient Merv was one of Islam's greatest cities until Mongol devastation—now a UNESCO archaeological park.
Winston Churchill called Uganda the 'pearl of Africa' for its lakes, gorillas, and equatorial highlands.
Idi Amin expelled the Asian community in 1972, many of whom resettled in the UK and reshaped British multiculturalism.
Lake Victoria—the continent's largest—touches Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania.
Kyivan Rus linked Vikings to Byzantium; Ukrainian identity reasserted itself through centuries of Polish, Russian, and Soviet rule.
The UK has been a leading military supporter after Russia's 2022 full-scale invasion, training soldiers and supplying equipment.
Chernobyl's 1986 disaster in northern Ukraine became a global symbol of nuclear risk and Soviet secrecy.
Seven emirates federated in 1971 as British protection ended; Abu Dhabi holds most oil, Dubai pivoted to trade and tourism.
The Burj Khalifa and palm-shaped islands showcase rapid desert urbanisation.
The UK maintains extensive defence cooperation, including basing and joint exercises in the Gulf.
The 1787 Constitution created a federal republic that grew from thirteen Atlantic colonies to a continent-spanning power.
The 'Special Relationship' with the UK—codified in WWII alliance—anchors NATO intelligence and nuclear cooperation.
Yellowstone, established in 1872, was the world's first national park.
Montevideo's art-deco and beef culture earned Uruguay the nickname 'Switzerland of South America' for stability and welfare.
It hosted the first FIFA World Cup final in 1930 and won twice in the tournament's early decades.
The UK once sought influence on the Plata estuary; today trade ties run through Mercosur and investment in services.
Samarkand's Registan was a Silk Road jewel; Timur (Tamerlane) built an empire from Central Asia to Anatolia.
Double landlocked, Uzbekistan ships goods overland through neighbours to reach seas.
Cotton monoculture under the Soviets drained the Aral Sea, one of history's worst environmental catastrophes.
Lake Maracaibo's lightning storms produce the 'Catatumbo lightning' phenomenon, visible many nights a year.
Oil wealth funded ambitious social programmes and, later, economic crisis under sanctions and mismanagement.
Simón Bolívar liberated much of Spanish South America; his legacy still polarises Venezuelan politics.
French Indochina and U.S. intervention left scars; Đổi Mới reforms since 1986 turned Vietnam into a manufacturing export hub.
Ha Long Bay's limestone karsts are a UNESCO icon of northern coastline.
The UK and Vietnam upgraded ties with a strategic partnership in the 2010s, linking development and education.
Sheba's legendary queen ties to Yemeni highland kingdoms that monopolised frankincense routes.
Sanaa's old city of tower houses is a UNESCO site in a nation shattered by civil war and humanitarian crisis.
Aden was a British protectorate hub until 1967; the UK still engages diplomatically on Red Sea security.
Victoria Falls—Mosi-oa-Tunya—straddles Zambia and Zimbabwe on the Zambezi, one of Earth's great waterfalls.
Copper built the economy; independence in 1964 brought Kenneth Kaunda's non-aligned leadership.
The UK is a development and mining investment partner and shares Commonwealth institutions with Zambia.
Great Zimbabwe's stone ruins testify to a medieval trade empire linked to Indian Ocean commerce.
Rhodesia's UDI (1965) led to a long guerrilla war; Zimbabwe emerged independent in 1980 with Robert Mugabe's long rule.
Hyperinflation in the late 2000s made Zimbabwe a textbook case in monetary collapse before dollarisation and reforms.
